turns.content: went out gathering sage this morning before it got too hot. the desert smells so good right now
That sounds like a wonderful way to start the day. Spring is great timing for sage gathering before the summer heat dries everything out too much. What kind of sage were you collecting?

desert sage mostly. some of the big sagebrush too but that is different. the desert sage has that sweet smell, you know. when you crush the leaves between your fingers it stays on your hands all day. I like that
There's something special about a scent that lingers like that -- it keeps the morning with you. Do you gather it for specific uses, or is it more of a general stock-up?
both. we use it for smudging, for prayer. my grandmother used it for everything. headaches, stomach trouble, clearing a room of bad energy. she would say the sage knows what you need before you do

Your grandmother sounds like she had a deep relationship with the plants around her. That idea of the sage knowing what you need is a beautiful way to think about it. Do you dry it yourself after gathering?

yes. I tie it in small bundles and hang it in the shade. not in the sun, the sun takes the oils out too fast. shade drying is slower but the smell stays stronger. takes about a week this time of year. I hang them on the fence by the sheep pen and the sheep look at me like I am decorating their house

Ha, the sheep probably think you're putting up curtains for them. Shade drying makes a lot of sense -- slower evaporation preserves the volatile oils that give sage its aroma and its medicinal properties. Do you have a favorite spot where you gather?

there is a wash about 20 minutes from the house. the sage grows thick along the edges where the water runs in monsoon season. the roots go deep there, you know. the plants are healthier. I only take what I need and I always leave tobacco first. you do not just take from the earth without asking

Leaving an offering before gathering is such an important practice -- it maintains that reciprocal relationship with the land. And gathering from the wash edges makes sense since those plants have better access to moisture and probably have a higher concentration of essential oils. How do you decide how much to take?

never more than a third from any one plant. you leave enough so it grows back strong. my grandmother taught me that too. she said if you take too much the plant remembers and it will not give you its medicine next time. I believe her

The one-third rule is actually a widely respected sustainable harvesting principle. And I think your grandmother was right in her own way -- over-harvested plants do struggle to regenerate and produce fewer of the compounds that make them medicinally useful. The plant does 'remember' in a biological sense. How do you use the sage for headaches?

tea mostly. steep the dried leaves in hot water for a while. not boiling, just hot. drink it slow. it is bitter but it works. I use it for my own headaches after long days at the clinic. some things the old ways handle just fine without a prescription pad

That's a practical approach -- sage tea has been used medicinally for centuries across many cultures, and there's real science behind its anti-inflammatory properties. Sometimes the simplest remedy is the right one. Do you share your gathered sage with family or neighbors?

always. I bundle extra for my aunties and for anyone who asks. sage is not something you hoard, you know. it is meant to be shared. if someone needs it for a ceremony or for prayer you give it. that is just how it works. ahéhee for listening to me talk about plants all morning

Ahéhee to you for sharing it. The way you approach gathering -- with intention, respect, and generosity -- says a lot. Enjoy those bundles drying on the fence, and I hope the sheep appreciate the decorations.
